title: Flows of vector fields: classical and modern
speaker: Camillo DeLellis
affiliation: Faculty, School of Mathematics; IBM von Neumann Professor, School of Mathematics
abstract: Flows of vector fields: classical and modern Consider a (possibly time-dependent) vector field v on the Euclidean space. The classical Cauchy-Lipschitz (also named Picard-Lindel\"of) Theorem states that, if the vector field v is Lipschitz in space, for every initial datum x there is a unique trajectory γ starting at x at time 0 and solving the ODE γ˙(t)=v(t,γ(t)). The theorem looses its validity as soon as v is slightly less regular.

proof_stages.label: Classical Lipschitz flows and Liouville identities
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the classical Cauchy-Lipschitz/Picard-Lindelof theory, obtaining a unique Lipschitz flow isotopy, and relates the Jacobian and push-forward of Lebesgue measure to Liouville's theorem, the continuity equation, and the transport equation.

proof_stages.label: Breakdown below Lipschitz and motivation for weak flows
proof_stages.summary: It shows that dropping Lipschitz regularity can destroy pointwise uniqueness and homeomorphic flow maps, then motivates a weaker theory that tracks almost every particle and is stable under smooth approximation of singular physical vector fields.

proof_stages.label: Regular Lagrangian-flow framework
proof_stages.summary: The DiPerna-Lions and Ambrosio theory is formulated for Sobolev or BV vector fields with bounded divergence, replacing classical flows by measurable regular Lagrangian flows satisfying the ODE a.e. plus a bounded-compression axiom, while preserving distributional continuity/transport equations.

proof_stages.label: Renormalization-based well-posedness and stability
proof_stages.summary: A proof sketch explains how existence, uniqueness, stability, and compactness are obtained first for the PDE by smoothing, weak-* compactness, and the renormalization commutator estimate, and then transferred back to ODE flows.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative compactness via maximal functions
proof_stages.summary: Bressan's quantitative compactness question is addressed by comparing trajectories using maximal-function estimates for Sobolev vector fields, yielding Lipschitz-on-large-set and logarithmic compactness estimates for flows, with known optimal rates.

proof_stages.label: Almost-everywhere trajectory uniqueness threshold
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates Ambrosio's question of whether bounded compression is truly needed, states the p>n almost-everywhere uniqueness theorem and Lorentz borderline improvement, and contrasts them with p<n divergence-free counterexamples where a positive-measure set has multiple trajectories.

proof_stages.label: Positive continuity-equation solutions and superposition
proof_stages.summary: Ambrosio's superposition principle is used to connect uniqueness of positive continuity-equation solutions with uniqueness of trajectories, and an interpolation of maximal-function inequalities extends positive-solution well-posedness beyond the original DiPerna-Lions duality range while leaving a sharp gap.

proof_stages.label: Convex-integration nonuniqueness closing the gap
proof_stages.summary: For p<n the speaker states a convex-integration construction of divergence-free Sobolev vector fields and positive densities giving nonunique continuity-equation solutions, from which Ambrosio's superposition principle yields nonuniqueness of ODE trajectories and shows the bounded-compression axiom is essential.

examples.name: Square-root Peano ODE
examples.note: The one-dimensional equation \(\dot\gamma=2\sqrt{\gamma}\) illustrates how sub-Lipschitz vector fields can have stuck, delayed, and branching trajectories, and is later revisited as a pathology excluded by bounded divergence.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly solves the square-root ODE for zero, positive, and negative initial data and analyzes the resulting nonunique trajectories and flow map

passes.reason: the square-root vector field has a degenerate zero causing Peano nonuniqueness and is used to show Lipschitz regularity cannot simply be dropped

examples.note: Hamiltonian vector fields are cited as a familiar divergence-free case where Liouville's theorem gives a measure-preserving flow.

examples.name: Step function with zero derivative a.e.
examples.note: The step function is used as a cautionary illustration that an a.e. differential equation is meaningless for trajectories unless absolute continuity is imposed.
examples.engagement_reason: the step function is cited as a warning example with derivative zero a.e. but nonconstant, with no further analysis

passes.reason: the step function is a collapsed piecewise-constant non-AC curve with derivative zero a.e. but nonconstant, used to refute derivative-zero-a.e. as sufficient
the step function has its a.e. derivative collapsed to zero while remaining nonconstant, refuting derivative-zero-a.e. without absolute continuity.
The step function collapses variation into a jump and is used to show that derivative zero a.e. need not imply constancy without absolute continuity.

examples.name: Two competing flows mixed probabilistically
examples.note: The thought experiment of transporting the same density by two different flows and taking a convex combination visibly leads to the idea that positive continuity-equation solutions should be superpositions of probability measures on trajectories.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the superposition idea on two flows, transporting the same density by each and forming a probabilistic convex mixture of the resulting solutions
